questions.question: What is the process of exchange of O2 from the atmosphere with CO2 produced by the cells commonly known as?
questions.explanation: This process of exchange of O2 from the atmosphere with CO2 produced by the cells is called breathing, commonly known as respiration.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: How do lower invertebrates like sponges, coelenterates, and flatworms exchange O2 with CO2?
options: Through a network of tracheal tubes
By simple diffusion over their entire body surface
Through specialised vascularised structures called gills
questions.explanation: Lower invertebrates like sponges, coelenterates, flatworms, etc., exchange O2 with CO2 by simple diffusion over their entire body surface.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which respiratory structure is used by earthworms to transport atmospheric air?
questions.explanation: Earthworms use their moist cuticle and insects have a network of tubes (tracheal tubes) to transport atmospheric air within the body.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the cartilaginous box in humans that helps in sound production called?
questions.explanation: Larynx is a cartilaginous box which helps in sound production and hence called the sound box.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: During swallowing, what structure covers the glottis to prevent the entry of food into the larynx?
questions.explanation: During swallowing glottis can be covered by a thin elastic cartilaginous flap called epiglottis to prevent the entry of food into the larynx.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: At which vertebral level does the human trachea divide into right and left primary bronchi?
questions.explanation: Trachea is a straight tube extending up to the mid-thoracic cavity, which divides at the level of 5th thoracic vertebra into a right and left primary bronchi.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What structural feature supports the tracheae, primary, secondary and tertiary bronchi, and initial bronchioles?
questions.explanation: The tracheae, primary, secondary and tertiary bronchi, and initial bronchioles are supported by incomplete cartilaginous rings.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The very thin, irregular-walled and vascularised bag-like structures at the end of terminal bronchioles are called:
questions.explanation: Each terminal bronchiole gives rise to a number of very thin, irregular-walled and vascularised bag-like structures called alveoli.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the function of the pleural fluid found between the double-layered pleura covering the lungs?
options: It facilitates the diffusion of oxygen.
It digests foreign particles entering the lungs.
It reduces friction on the lung-surface.
It actively pumps air into the alveoli.
questions.explanation: We have two lungs which are covered by a double layered pleura, with pleural fluid between them. It reduces friction on the lung-surface.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which parts of the respiratory system constitute the exchange part?
options: External nostrils up to the terminal bronchioles
questions.explanation: The part starting with the external nostrils up to the terminal bronchioles constitute the conducting part whereas the alveoli and their ducts form the respiratory or exchange part of the respiratory system.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the conducting part of the respiratory system?
options: Actual diffusion of O2 and CO2
Bringing the air to body temperature
questions.explanation: The conducting part transports the atmospheric air to the alveoli, clears it from foreign particles, humidifies and also brings the air to body temperature. Exchange part is the site of actual diffusion of O2 and CO2.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Anatomically, the thoracic chamber is formed dorsally by the:
questions.explanation: The thoracic chamber is formed dorsally by the vertebral column, ventrally by the sternum, laterally by the ribs and on the lower side by the dome-shaped diaphragm.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Under normal conditions, inspiration occurs when the intra-pulmonary pressure is:
questions.explanation: Inspiration can occur if the pressure within the lungs (intra-pulmonary pressure) is less than the atmospheric pressure...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The contraction of the diaphragm initiates inspiration by increasing the volume of the thoracic chamber in the:
questions.explanation: Inspiration is initiated by the contraction of diaphragm which increases the volume of thoracic chamber in the antero-posterior axis.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which muscles contract to lift up the ribs and the sternum, increasing the thoracic chamber volume in the dorso-ventral axis?
questions.explanation: The contraction of external inter-costal muscles lifts up the ribs and the sternum causing an increase in the volume of the thoracic chamber in the dorso-ventral axis.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: On an average, a healthy human breathes how many times per minute?
questions.explanation: On an average, a healthy human breathes 12-16 times/minute.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which clinical instrument is used to estimate the volume of air involved in breathing movements?
questions.explanation: The volume of air involved in breathing movements can be estimated by using a spirometer which helps in clinical assessment of pulmonary functions.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the approximate volume of air inspired or expired during a normal respiration (Tidal Volume) in a healthy man?
questions.explanation: Tidal Volume (TV): Volume of air inspired or expired during a normal respiration. It is approx. 500 mL.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The additional volume of air a person can inspire by a forcible inspiration is called Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV). What is its average value?
questions.explanation: Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV): A person can inspire by a forcible inspiration. This averages 2500 mL to 3000 mL.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What does the Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) represent?
options: Volume of air remaining in the lungs after a forcible expiration.
Additional volume of air a person can expire by a forcible expiration.
Total volume of air a person can expire after a normal inspiration.
Volume of air inspired during normal respiration.
questions.explanation: Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV): A person can expire by a forcible expiration. This averages 1000 mL to 1100 mL.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Even after a forcible expiration, some air remains in the lungs. What is this volume called?
questions.explanation: Residual Volume (RV): Volume of air remaining in the lungs even after a forcible expiration. This averages 1100 mL to 1200 mL.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following capacities is calculated as Tidal Volume + Inspiratory Reserve Volume (TV + IRV)?
questions.explanation: Inspiratory Capacity (IC): Total volume of air a person can inspire after a normal expiration. This includes tidal volume and inspiratory reserve volume (TV+IRV).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: How is Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) calculated?
questions.explanation: Functional Residual Capacity (FRC): Volume of air that will remain in the lungs after a normal expiration. This includes ERV+RV.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The maximum volume of air a person can breathe in after a forced expiration is defined as:
questions.explanation: Vital Capacity (VC): The maximum volume of air a person can breathe in after a forced expiration. This includes ERV, TV and IRV...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Total Lung Capacity (TLC) is the sum of which respiratory volumes?
questions.explanation: Total Lung Capacity (TLC): Total volume of air accommodated in the lungs at the end of a forced inspiration. This includes RV, ERV, TV and IRV or vital capacity + residual volume.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the partial pressure of oxygen (pO2) in the atmospheric air?
questions.explanation: Table 14.1 Partial Pressures... Atmospheric Air: O2 is 159 mm Hg.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: According to the data, what is the partial pressure of oxygen (pO2) in the alveoli?
questions.explanation: Table 14.1 shows that the partial pressure of Oxygen (pO2) in the Alveoli is 104 mm Hg.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the partial pressure of carbon dioxide (pCO2) in deoxygenated blood?
questions.explanation: Table 14.1 shows that the partial pressure of CO2 in deoxygenated blood is 45 mm Hg.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: How much higher is the solubility of CO2 compared to that of O2?
questions.explanation: As the solubility of CO2 is 20-25 times higher than that of O2, the amount of CO2 that can diffuse through the diffusion membrane per unit difference in partial pressure is much higher...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three major layers that make up the diffusion membrane?
questions.explanation: The diffusion membrane is made up of three major layers... namely, the thin squamous epithelium of alveoli, the endothelium of alveolar capillaries and the basement substance...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What percentage of O2 is transported by RBCs in the blood?
questions.explanation: About 97 per cent of O2 is transported by RBCs in the blood. The remaining 3 per cent of O2 is carried in a dissolved state through the plasma.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: How is the majority of carbon dioxide (about 70 per cent) transported in the blood?
options: Bound directly to white blood cells
questions.explanation: Nearly 20-25 per cent of CO2 is transported by RBCs whereas 70 per cent of it is carried as bicarbonate.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the maximum number of O2 molecules that each haemoglobin molecule can carry?
questions.explanation: Each haemoglobin molecule can carry a maximum of four molecules of O2.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: When percentage saturation of haemoglobin with O2 is plotted against pO2, what is the shape of the resulting Oxygen dissociation curve?
questions.explanation: A sigmoid curve is obtained when percentage saturation of haemoglobin with O2 is plotted against the pO2. This curve is called the Oxygen dissociation curve...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following conditions in the alveoli is favorable for the formation of oxyhaemoglobin?
options: High pO2 and lesser H+ concentration
questions.explanation: In the alveoli, where there is high pO2, low pCO2, lesser H+ concentration and lower temperature, the factors are all favourable for the formation of oxyhaemoglobin...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In the tissues, which conditions favor the dissociation of oxygen from oxyhaemoglobin?
options: High pO2, low pCO2, and low temperature
Low pO2, high pCO2, high H+ concentration, and higher temperature
Low pO2, low pCO2, and low temperature
High pO2, high pCO2, and low H+ concentration
questions.explanation: ...whereas in the tissues, where low pO2, high pCO2, high H+ concentration and higher temperature exist, the conditions are favourable for dissociation of oxygen from the oxyhaemoglobin.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Under normal physiological conditions, how much O2 can 100 ml of oxygenated blood deliver to the tissues?
questions.explanation: Every 100 ml of oxygenated blood can deliver around 5 ml of O2 to the tissues under normal physiological conditions.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In what form is 20-25 per cent of CO2 carried by haemoglobin?
questions.explanation: CO2 is carried by haemoglobin as carbamino-haemoglobin (about 20-25 per cent).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which enzyme found in high concentrations in RBCs facilitates the reaction of CO2 and H2O to form H2CO3?
questions.explanation: RBCs contain a very high concentration of the enzyme, carbonic anhydrase and minute quantities of the same is present in the plasma too.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: At the tissue site, what happens to the CO2 that diffuses into the blood due to high partial pressure?
options: It binds exclusively to plasma proteins.
questions.explanation: At the tissue site where partial pressure of CO2 is high due to catabolism, CO2 diffuses into blood (RBCs and plasma) and forms HCO3- and H+.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Approximately how much CO2 is delivered to the alveoli by every 100 ml of deoxygenated blood?
questions.explanation: Every 100 ml of deoxygenated blood delivers approximately 4 ml of CO2 to the alveoli.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which specific region of the brain contains the respiratory rhythm centre primarily responsible for regulating respiration?
questions.explanation: A specialised centre present in the medulla region of the brain called respiratory rhythm centre is primarily responsible for this regulation.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the function of the pneumotaxic centre located in the pons region of the brain?
options: It initiates the first breath in a newborn.
It moderates the functions of the respiratory rhythm centre, specifically reducing the duration of inspiration.
It continuously measures oxygen levels in the blood.
It stimulates the diaphragm to contract maximally.
questions.explanation: Another centre present in the pons region of the brain called pneumotaxic centre can moderate the functions of the respiratory rhythm centre. Neural signal from this centre can reduce the duration of inspiration and thereby alter the respiratory rate.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The chemosensitive area adjacent to the rhythm centre is highly sensitive to which of the following substances?
questions.explanation: A chemosensitive area is situated adjacent to the rhythm centre which is highly sensitive to CO2 and hydrogen ions.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Receptors associated with which structures can recognise changes in CO2 and H+ concentration and send signals to the rhythm centre?
questions.explanation: Receptors associated with aortic arch and carotid artery also can recognise changes in CO2 and H+ concentration and send necessary signals to the rhythm centre for remedial actions.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which gas plays a quite insignificant role in the regulation of the respiratory rhythm?
questions.explanation: The role of oxygen in the regulation of respiratory rhythm is quite insignificant.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which respiratory disorder is characterised by difficulty in breathing and wheezing due to inflammation of bronchi and bronchioles?
questions.explanation: Asthma is a difficulty in breathing causing wheezing due to inflammation of bronchi and bronchioles.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Emphysema is a chronic disorder in which the respiratory surface is decreased. What is a major cause of this disease?
questions.explanation: Emphysema is a chronic disorder in which alveolar walls are damaged due to which respiratory surface is decreased. One of the major causes of this is cigarette smoking.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Long exposure to dust in certain industries like stone-breaking can lead to serious lung damage. What is this specific condition of tissue proliferation called?
questions.explanation: Occupational Respiratory Disorders... Long exposure can give rise to inflammation leading to fibrosis (proliferation of fibrous tissues) and thus causing serious lung damage.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In the mechanism of inspiration, what is the combined effect of the contraction of the diaphragm and external inter-costals?
options: Decreasing the thoracic volume, leading to higher intra-pulmonary pressure
Increasing the thoracic volume, leading to lower intra-pulmonary pressure
No change in thoracic volume, but lower intra-pulmonary pressure
Decreasing the thoracic volume, leading to lower intra-pulmonary pressure
questions.explanation: The overall increase in the thoracic volume causes a similar increase in pulmonary volume. An increase in pulmonary volume decreases the intra-pulmonary pressure... which forces the air from outside to move into the lungs, i.e., inspiration.
questions.subject: Biology
